resources.string:
Recusrively scanning in:
- WheelLog/
- EUC World/

from: %1$s

Ensure your logs are in those folders.
PDF saved — tap SHARE to send it
CSV saved — tap SHARE to send it
Archive saved — tap SHARE to send it
These parameters are used to segregate R_batt from R_mosfet during analysis.
• Coeff temp : +1%%/°C is usual for MOSFETs
Name from logs — cannot be overridden
Cells in parallel per group (1P, 2P, 3P…) — default 1
What this tool does — and doesn\'t do

EUC SoH analyzes your ride logs (WheelLog / EUC World CSV) over thousands of kilometers to track the long-term health of your battery pack and power electronics (MOSFETs). It derives empirical indicators — equivalent resistance, voltage sag, thermal stress — and detects degradation trends or abnormal events using statistical thresholds and CUSUM change-point detection.
It does not predict an exact remaining lifespan.
It does not replace an oscilloscope, a lab charge/discharge cycle, or a controlled BMS diagnostic.
It does not compare your wheel to other riders — each wheel is its own reference.
Indicators are relative, not absolute: a high R_eq does not mean the battery is bad in isolation, only that it has drifted from its own baseline.
How to act on the results
Alarms use three levels based on how far a metric has drifted from its per-wheel baseline (mean ± σ computed on the full ride history):
A clear abnormal deviation. Reduce aggressive riding sessions (high current, low battery), inspect connectors and cells, and consider professional maintenance before continuing long trips.
An early drift worth watching. Increase monitoring frequency. No immediate action required, but plan ahead.
Metrics within the expected range for this wheel\'s history.

When a danger alarm fires on R_eq or R_batt: avoid riding near empty battery, avoid sustained high-current conditions (steep hills, heavy load at low SoC). Consult the CUSUM and trend charts to see when the drift started.
How nominal pack resistance is estimated

EUC SoH infers the nominal resistance of your battery pack from the maximum pack voltage recorded in your logs, without requiring you to enter the exact cell model. The number of cells in series (S) is derived from the maximum voltage. A typical new-cell DC internal resistance (r_cell) is then looked up from public data for 18650 and 21700 chemistries.
R_pack_nominal = S × r_cell / P\n\nwhere S = series cell count, P = parallel cell count (configurable, default 1)

This nominal value is used as an absolute safety net: if R_eq_median exceeds a configurable multiple of R_pack_nominal (e.g. 1.8×), an alarm is raised regardless of the statistical baseline. This catches the case where the wheel was already degraded when you started logging.

A 100V wheel typically has 24S cells. With r_cell = 80 mΩ (fresh 21700) and P = 1: R_nominal = 24 × 0.080 / 1 = 1.92 Ω. A measured R_eq_median of 3.5 Ω would be ~1.8× nominal, triggering the absolute alarm.
The P value can be set in the MOSFET config dialog (field "N parallel cells"). It does not affect resistance computation — only the R_nominal alarm threshold.
Key metrics and how to read them
Median equivalent resistance of the full pack (battery + MOSFETs + wiring) across all high-load samples in a ride. Main global health indicator.

R_eq_median captures all drift regardless of its origin — battery, MOSFETs, or wiring. A sustained upward trend means the circuit is degrading, but it does not tell you what is failing. To attribute the drift, cross-reference with other metrics: rising SAG and R_batt point to the cells; exploding temp_board_max with stable SAG points to the MOSFETs. R_eq_median is the primary alarm trigger — the other metrics explain it.

Battery resistance isolated from MOSFETs and wiring, corrected to 25°C. Only available when MOSFET parameters are configured. Without them, this metric is not shown — R_eq_median_25C is the closest available value but it includes the full circuit.
Slow increase = cell ageing. Fast increase = possible cell failure, bad contact, or BMS issue. Flat while R_eq rises = MOSFET degradation is the dominant cause.

Static proxy for MOSFET bridge resistance at operating temperature. Computed as R_ds(on)_25°C (from the datasheet value you entered) scaled by a linear thermal coefficient. The reference value R_ds(on)_25°C is fixed — it does not update as the component ages.

Because R_ds(on)_25°C is a fixed constant in the model, R_mosfet_hot does not directly track MOSFET ageing. What it captures is the thermal variation around that fixed baseline.
Real MOSFET degradation (increased on-state resistance due to oxide wear, bond fatigue, etc.) will appear in R_eq_median rising over time — not in R_mosfet_hot alone.
The diagnostic signature of MOSFET failure is: R_eq_median trending up + temp_board_max increasing at comparable power conditions + SAG remaining relatively stable (cells still healthy). If SAG and R_batt also rise, the degradation is more widespread.
95th percentile and maximum voltage sag (Vidle − V) recorded under high load. Captures worst-case ohmic drops during a ride.
High sag = pack cannot maintain voltage under load, which shrinks the firmware\'s safety margin before cut-off. Dangerous at low SoC or in cold conditions.
Minimum pack voltage reached under high-load conditions. Reflects the remaining margin before the firmware\'s low-voltage protection triggers.
Decreasing over time = safety margin shrinking. Value dropping below your firmware\'s cut-off threshold means a sudden power cut is possible under hard acceleration.
Maximum controller/board temperature recorded during a ride. Used as a proxy for MOSFET junction temperature.
Upward trend at similar power conditions = increasing thermal resistance in the power stage (degraded thermal interface, aging MOSFETs). High values increase failure risk.
95th percentile and maximum battery current. Characterizes electrical stress on the pack cells and MOSFETs.
Alarms on these metrics indicate unusually aggressive riding sessions (high acceleration, steep terrain). High currents accelerate both battery and MOSFET degradation.
EUC SoH is open-source research software. Results are indicative only. Always prioritize safety and consult a qualified technician for maintenance decisions.

Cumulative integral of phase current squared (∫ I² dt) summed over all rides, normalized by distance. Measures the total electrical energy dissipated as heat in the power stage over the wheel\'s lifetime.

Each point is the thermal dose for one ride — it does not accumulate between rides. A ride with unusually high sustained current will produce a high point relative to the trend. The chart also shows a cumulative line (right axis) that sums the dose from ride 1 to ride N, giving a picture of total thermal stress accumulated over the wheel\'s lifetime. Use the per-ride points to spot aggressive sessions; use the cumulative line to assess long-term load history.

R_eq_median fluctuates around a flat baseline across thousands of kilometres. Points stay inside the green–orange band. No upward trend. This is the expected signature of a healthy pack with consistent riding conditions.

R_eq_median shows a sustained upward trend from ~0.12 Ω to above 0.40 Ω over 27 000 km, eventually crossing the orange (+1σ) then the red Danger threshold. The last sessions spike sharply above 0.40 Ω. This pattern indicates accelerating pack degradation — the rate of increase matters as much as the absolute value.
Red dashed line: Danger threshold (µ + 2σ or absolute)

For each ride, the app selects samples where speed > 20 km/h and battery current is in a meaningful range. For each such sample it computes:
R_eq = (V_idle − V_measured) / |I_battery|
where V_idle is a local estimate of the no-load voltage, rebuilt from rest segments scattered throughout the ride (not a single value for the whole ride). R_eq_median is the median of all those per-sample values. It reflects the combined resistance of battery cells + MOSFETs + wiring.

V_idle is not a fixed number. The app identifies segments where |I| < 3 A for at least 5 s with no abrupt voltage change (dV/dt < 0.5 V/s). It interpolates a smooth profile between those rest points. This gives a local reference that follows SoC naturally, without needing a precise SoC measurement.
R_mosfet and R_batt — splitting the resistance

R_eq_median is always computed. It is then normalized to 25°C via Arrhenius to give reqMedian25C — this is the temperature-corrected equivalent resistance of the full circuit (cells + MOSFETs + wiring).
R_batt (the battery resistance isolated from the rest) is only computed when MOSFET parameters are configured: R_mosfet(T) = R_ds(on)_25°C × (1 + α × (T − 25)) + R_wiring R_batt = max(0, R_eq_median − R_mosfet(T_board)) R_batt_median_25C = Arrhenius correction of R_batt
Without MOSFET config, R_batt and R_batt_median_25C are not shown. The code does not assume R_eq ≈ R_batt — it simply does not attempt the split.

Battery internal resistance depends on temperature. To make values comparable across seasons and conditions, R_batt is normalized to 25°C using the Arrhenius law:
R_25°C = R_measured / exp((Ea/R_gas) × (1/T_meas − 1/298.15))
Ea defaults to 20 kJ/mol (typical Li-ion). The app can auto-calibrate Ea from your history if the temperature span in your logs is large enough (≥ 5°C).

This metric integrates the square of the phase current over the ride duration:
I²_int = (∫ I_phase² dt) / T_ride
The integral is computed with the trapezoid rule using actual timestamps when available, or a 0.1 s fallback. Squaring the current makes it proportional to Joule heating — doubling the current quadruples the heat. Normalizing by ride duration makes a long calm ride and a short aggressive ride comparable on a per-ride basis.
The chart displays both the per-ride dose (left axis) and a cumulative sum from ride 1 to N (right axis, dashed grey line).
I_phase2_Int is only available when your logger records phase current (EUC World or WheelLog with that column enabled).
